item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Has been and will always be one of my favourite SCPs of all time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loving the Zann reference! My second favourite Lovecraft piece. From the Rue d'Auseil, +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes it should! Thank you, this is fixed, along with a quick cleanup of the description. No need to invoke superstrings anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Living plants and animals will appear to becoming younger

shouldn't this say "become"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An SCP from where I live!!! EEEEEE

Ok, enough fangirling about that. Otherwise I found it an ineteresting concept =D
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ils coopéraient ensemble, as it were.

It's a funny thing - when an idea's time has come…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This time… I would chalk it up to happy coincidence, but one thing I loved about our MTF contest team was that we all just got on the same wavelength and really got our connections down. Ils coopéraient ensemble, as it were.

We all knew Hennessy had died, even though he was still alive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I seem to keep coming back to this, but a thought just struck me. This exchange between Agents Zhao and Hennessy in A Convincing Lyre:

"See," said Zhao, ignoring Mike's mock scowl, "you'd be more use to us than him! And we need someone new to join before we all die off." She looked at Hennessy and signed Sorry.

Have to go some time, he signed back cheerfully, before walking into the equipment room.

takes on a whole new layer of meaning, based on the events described in the final note of this SCP. Was that deliberate, or just another happy coincidence?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, that's just a happy coincidence. If there's any other SCP I was specifically thinking of, it was SCP-407, but SCP-2407 was already taken.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A quick question - is the choice of number in any way related to SCP-224, which appears to have acousti-temporal effects in the other direction?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not thinking of anything nearly as physically distant as moving back in with your parents when the song makes you younger, though. I'm thinking of very localized movements, like walking within or out of a room.

Let's consider Agent Zhao. Agent Zhao went into the cave with the musicians and paid them to undo three years of aging. Then they started playing. If Zhao's movements are reversed when the song is played (as well as Zhao's aging), then the process of her going into the cave (<1 day before the song) should have finished reversing long before the process of her aging for the past 3 years (3 years before the song). Zhao walks in, she stops, some time passes, the music starts, a much shorter interval of time passes, and Zhao begins to reverse-walk out again.

She stops un-walking after crossing the 40 dB threshold, but she stops un-aging at the same time, and she un-ages at the same rate as she un-walks. So the earliest moment she can possibly be reverted to by the song is the moment when she entered what would later be the >40dB area of effect. (Even moving the effect to follow her won't help much, since she goes in reverse at millions of times the rate at which she originally traveled.) If this is how the song works, it's much more limited in effect than I originally thought- you can only reverse people and objects through time periods when they were stationary or barely moving. It'll raise the dead if they're still in the spot where they died, it'll fix broken objects if they're still in the place where they broke, but it won't de-age anything past the last time it traveled more than half a kilometer or so (unless you have a hell of a speaker and don't mind reverting large swathes of the world). If the musicians were using this to de-age their clients, they wouldn't have had much success, and Zhao would hardly have forgotten anything when she listened, let alone observed visible signs of reversed aging.

Alternatively, what I was imagining from all the text before Hennessey's note was that the song only de-ages matter, it doesn't reverse actions. With this effect, Zhao could stay in the cave listening and getting younger as long as she liked- if she listened long enough to reverse three years' aging, the end result would be an identical Zhao to the one that existed three years ago, suddenly finding herself in a cave outside Gamya wearing her field gear (or the raw materials thereof) instead of wherever she actually was three years beforehand in whatever she was actually wearing at that moment. This makes sense of the musicians' business, but doesn't fit with Hennessey's story of the cave collapse.

+ I like the article, though, so here's one possible explanation for both effects.
- The cave entrance was blocked when Hennessey died.

The musicians subsequently played from inside, but the surveillance camera must have been on the outside, or else it wouldn't have any footage of the incident at all. So the audio from the footage is a very poor record of what the musicians played to reverse the cave collapse. Plus, Hennessey's deaf, and after Eta-11 learned what the song could do, they probably wouldn't play raw audio of an incident where it was used at all.

Hennessey doesn't actually know what the musicians did inside the cave to get the collapse to reverse itself. Especially since only one of them survived engagement with Eta-11, and he ended up thoroughly de-aged. Maybe the musicians knew multiple ways to play the song, to get different variations of its effect. The version that reversed Agent Zhao and their other customers through time without reversing them through space wouldn't have returned the rubble to the roof. The version that returned the rubble to the roof wouldn't have reversed Agent Zhao's aging without first reversing her through space and out of the effective range. So it would make sense, if the musicians knew both versions, for them to use one for their fountain-of-youth business and the other only for emergencies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, some movement is necessary, of course, while rewinding happens. A refilling nail hole or a child shrinking or a memory unmaking itself all involves things moving into a previously extant configuration. Of course, you don't, say, move back in with your parents and re-enroll in elementary school, because the rewinding effect isn't there. Sound dissipates the further away you get from its origin.

But, you could see the full effect if the entire affected area is in the range of effect of the sound. Hennessey wouldn't, for example, head back into Garnya and eventually fly back to the Foundation base. However, rocks returning to their location when the original location (a couple meters away on the cave mouth wall) is also being rewound can happen.

I suppose, though, if the song were played everywhere around the world (or at least everywhere where people were), you could see people and vehicles rushing back to their original locations. But what would it take to have a song played everywhere?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is very cool on its own (I have so many interesting questions for experiments), and using it to do something new with SCP-1668 is downright inspired. The one thing that bugs me about it is, does the sound only revert materials to previous physical states, or does it actually rewind time within the area of effect, or something in between?

It's pretty clear that when it affects a person, that person becomes younger and loses recent memories, and it also seems like they don't reverse their actions- subjects don't walk backward out of the room where the music is playing, for instance. And construction is described being unmade, but again, in terms of the materials reverting, not the structure being un-constructed. Wooden planks lose their nail holes, welded beams lose their welds, but the nails don't reverse the hammering process and the material doesn't get picked up and carried off by the backwards echoes of construction workers. At the end of the piece, everything affected is in the same place as it was at the start, just a different material state.

But in Hennessey's note at the end, he describes the cave collapse totally rewinding itself- not only did the byproducts of the explosion turn back into explosives and Hennessey's dead tissues turn back into living ones, but the materials rearranged themselves into previous locations as well. The fallen rocks flew back up into the cave ceiling, the fragmented explosives imploded into complete units, and Hennessey's crushed parts assembled into a viable body plan.

Is that part of the phenomenon normal for SCP-2402, or is it some kind of additional anomalous effect only seen in that one instance? Based on the procedures and descriptions, I'm guessing it's not normal, but the article proper doesn't mention it at all, even to say "this one time, something different happened; we don't know why and we haven't seen anything like it since."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was at a neutral vote until I hit that last note. That narrative convinced me to upvote the piece as a whole, though this could have been streamlined considerably without losing anything from the piece. All of this is to say that I generally concur with the sentiments upthread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, now it makes sense. I knew I'd missed something, thanks for clarifying that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so much for your thoughts!

If there's any confusion, it's probably because the temporal effect is acoustic in nature. The sound has to be generated for the music to rewind time. As a result, you can imagine the music in your head, write it down, record it digitally without playing it, and there will be no effect and you can still know the music.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this, such a clever idea. Your writing is great as always, and the last log with Hennessey was also terrific. I really liked how, for a skip that does something so extraordinary, it is essentially useless - you can make yourself grow younger, over and over, ad infinitum, but each time you lose your memories. But what is eternal life without memory? You're essentially just starting over, without anything you might have learned, or the benefit of your experiences.

I'm reminded of a passage by Marcel Proust, who postulated (paraphrasing alert!) that if the soul is real, and that we existed before we were born and will continue to exist after we die, then that still does not mean we have eternal life - because we don't remember it, all we're actually doing is living a series of successive lives, starting over from scratch, again and again, forever. Anyway, that's what your skip made me think of, sorry if that came out all pretentious-like. :P

Aside from that, some aspects of the Erich Zann collapsible were a little confusing to me (such as how Zann remembered the music when it's supposed to make you forget as it makes you grow younger, or how Mike was able to make an audio file of the music when it is supposedly impossible to duplicate, or what Mike's note at the end is supposed to mean), but I suspect that was just me missing something.

Enthusiastically upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fine humor it is, Conwell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with what sirpudding here. Both titles are great, but the current one really is the best choice for your theme. My above comments were just me attempting humor. :I

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Both titles are really good, but the current one seems to reinforce the themes of your overall story better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh. My working title was indeed Sonic Youth, but given the theme of the piece, I went with the title that evokes reminiscing. But hey, who here would prefer the title being Sonic Youth?

ETA: And, just because I'm on a Jamin Winans kick again, here's SPIN.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … I rescind my previous statement. The current title is wasted potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, the skips title is pretty clever.

I still can't believe WJS didn't go with "Sonic Youth"!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A neat concept for an SCP article. I agree with some of the previous comments in this thread, that last note is really what sells this skip.

Also, the skips title is pretty clever. Kudos there as well.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very very nice. It has been quite a while since I was so hooked on an SCP from the very beginning. Just reading the containment procedures I had to keep asking "Why only old soundproofing?" and things like that. I was expecting the more cliché, destroys anything too technologically advanced, type of crap and was happily surprised. Many thumbs up! (though Im only allowed to give a +1)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you very much, psul.

I think the big thing for me is that as I design the story, I keep asking myself, "How does that work? And how does that work?" as well as "What are the implications of that?" As I keep asking, the story does get bigger and bigger, but more connected as reasons and meanings compound. You end up asking questions like, "What are the scientific implications of moving items back and forth through time? Do we just throw the philosophical construct of Time's Arrow out the window? Does this mean believing in a timeline is fruitless? What if you regressed to a point where you never did certain acts, but still had to be held responsible for them? How would you carry the guilt of actions you never committed?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of the things I've really enjoyed about this contest is the chance to work with other writers and learn from their different approaches.

One of my instincts as a writer and editor is to pare back and refine ideas. Something I've been really impressed with, WJS, is your willingness to use the opposite approach - to go big, and add connections and depth to the initial idea. It has characterised your writing in the On Mount Golgotha canon generally, and this is another great example. Every draft I read had another layer added, and it has built up into something that I really like. It's fantastic to see a bit of how that works, and to think about how to use it myself in future work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: These are some of the the best crossslinks I have seen, and the final note is just perfect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I say again, great development of the idea, expands so well into "On Mount Golgotha," and keeps me interested 'til the very last moment. Nice execution, managing to differentiate itself from other sound/compulsive SCPs very well.

by the way, you didn't have to thank me, WrongJohn… always happy to help!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Edit fail. I used ABC as the number placeholder. I missed it.

ETA: Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, I do not know what to make of this. I've been liking this MTF's entries so far, but this one is pretty much your standard anomalous music piece. Until that final note. I'm thinking I should upvote overall, but it's a very tentative upvote. There's really not a lot here, it's all about that final note.

Regular testing of SCP-ABC's effects

what D:

item.title: Duuuuude
item.content:encoded:
This is really good. I keep telling people skips should tell a story, and this one really does. Emotional, interesting, and well written.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That last note packs a fucking hella punch. The crosslinks also work very very well in favor of this. Adds depth to both SCPs referenced, I feel.

item.title: Author's Post
item.content:encoded:
It hurts my brain to think of all the stupid things I've said,
And if I could change the future, I would change the past instead.

— Oingo Boingo, "Change"

Here is the SCP entry for Team Site-19 String Quartet, covering MTF Eta-11 ("Savage Beasts"). Many thanks to DrStranger, Zmax15, and my teammates CumaeanSibyl, Smapti, and psul for reviewing this idea.

If you're interested in the rest of the story of MTF Eta-11 ("Savage Beasts"), please check out the following articles, listed in suggested order:

  • A Convincing Lyre
  • An Impenitent Thief
  • Project Proposal 2014-2112: "Dreams of Failure"
  • SCP-2402
  • Enjoy the Silence
  • The Musical Suspects

It's been great working with my team on getting these together. I know this isn't the last we'll hear of Eta-11.

ETA: Image is from Wikipedia and is public domain.
